faq.title: Frequently Asked Questions
faq.subtitle: Everything you need to know about Seizn

questions.q: What is AI Memory and how is it different from RAG?
questions.a: AI Memory is persistent storage that remembers user preferences, facts, and context across sessions. Unlike RAG which retrieves static documents, Seizn dynamically extracts and updates memories from conversations, creating a living knowledge base.
All data is stored on secure servers in the US (AWS) with end-to-end encryption. We comply with GDPR and CCPA. You can request complete data deletion at any time.
1) Sign up free, 2) Create API key, 3) Install SDK (npm install @seizn/sdk), 4) Add memories with 3 lines of code.

questions.q: What happens if I exceed my plan limits?
questions.a: We notify you at 80%. If you exceed, API calls continue at reduced priority. Upgrade anytime for instant limit increase.
Yes. Delete individual memories, bulk delete by tags, or clear all. Deleted data is removed within 24 hours.
Any model: GPT-4, Claude, Gemini, Llama. SDKs for JS/TS, Python, REST API. Works with LangChain, LlamaIndex, Vercel AI.
Free plan is permanent: 10,000 memories, 1,000 API calls/month. No credit card needed.
Under 50ms average. Hybrid vector + graph search with optimized indexes.

whySeizn.title: Why Seizn vs LangChain + Pinecone?
whySeizn.subtitle: Stop gluing together fragmented tools. Get everything you need in one integrated stack.
whySeizn.tracingDesc: Every request is traced by default. Run evals, detect regressions, and debug production issues without adding LangSmith or custom logging.
whySeizn.autopilotDesc: Set a latency or cost budget, and Autopilot automatically chooses the optimal retrieval strategy. No manual tuning required.
whySeizn.governanceDesc: PII detection, GDPR-compliant forget, and complete audit trails. Built for teams who need compliance, not bolted on later.
whySeizn.lessGlueDesc: No more juggling LangChain + Pinecone + LangSmith + custom PII filters. One SDK, one dashboard, one bill.

authentication.title: Authentication
authentication.description: All API requests require an API key passed in the
authentication.securityText: Keep your API keys secret. Never expose them in client-side code. Use environment variables or a backend proxy.

postMemories.description: Add a new memory to the user's memory store.
postMemories.memoryType: string - Type: fact, preference, experience, relationship, instruction
postMemories.sessionId: string - Session ID for session-scoped memories
postMemories.agentId: string - Agent ID for agent-scoped memories

extract.description: Extract and store memories from a conversation using AI.
extract.conversation: string (required) - The conversation text to extract memories from
extract.model: string - AI model: haiku (faster) or sonnet (better) (default: haiku)
extract.autoStore: boolean - Automatically store extracted memories (default: true)

query.description: Get AI-generated responses using relevant memories as context (RAG).
query.queryParam: string (required) - The user's question or prompt
query.model: string - AI model: haiku or sonnet (default: haiku)
query.topK: number - Number of memories to use as context (default: 5)
query.includeMemories: boolean - Include used memories in response (default: true)

faqPage.title: Frequently Asked Questions
faqPage.subtitle: Common questions about Seizn Memory API, from getting started to advanced operations.
faqPage.stillHaveQuestionsDesc: Check out our documentation or get in touch with our support team.

items.question: What is Seizn Memory and what problem does it solve?
items.answer: Seizn Memory is an AI memory infrastructure that provides persistent, searchable memory for AI applications. Unlike vector databases that only store/search vectors, Seizn includes the full product layer: memory extraction, policy management, key management, deletion, audit logs, and SDKs. It solves the problem of maintaining context across AI sessions and enabling personalized AI experiences.

items.question: How is Seizn different from vector databases like Pinecone or Weaviate?
items.answer: Vector databases are storage/search infrastructure for vectors. Seizn is a complete memory system built on top of that, providing: automatic memory extraction from conversations, memory type classification, namespace/scope management, API key rotation, audit logging, SDKs, and governance features. Think of it as 'memory infrastructure' vs 'search infrastructure'.

items.question: Do I need RAG to use Seizn?
items.answer: No. The most common starting point is: store memory -> search -> inject into prompt. RAG (automatic context composition + response generation) is the next step. You can start simple and add complexity as needed.

items.question: What's the fastest way to build a PoC?
items.answer: 1) POST /api/memories to store a user preference. 2) GET /api/memories to search. 3) Inject the results into your LLM prompt. 4) Later, add /api/extract for auto-extraction and /api/query for memory-augmented responses.

items.question: What should I store in /api/memories?
items.answer: Store information that remains valid across conversations: preferences (tone, language, format), facts (job, tools, project structure), instructions ("always summarize in tables"), relationships ("Alice is the team lead"). Avoid storing temporary or session-specific data unless using session scope.

items.question: What data should I NOT store?
items.answer: Never store: passwords, API keys, tokens, session cookies (auth info), SSN, passport numbers, bank accounts (PII), credit card info (payment data). For temporary data, use session scope with TTL if needed.

items.question: Why is namespace important?
items.answer: Namespace separates memories by project/tenant/environment. Without it, data gets mixed, search quality drops, and deletion/export becomes painful. Recommended: 'org:acme/app:chat/env:prod' or 'project:myapp/env:staging'. Never use just 'default' in production.

items.question: When should I use scope (user/session/agent)?
items.answer: user: Preferences that apply to a user globally. session: Goals or context valid only for this conversation. agent: Rules specific to one agent in a multi-agent system. Using scope properly reduces prompt length and improves response consistency.

items.question: What are memory_types and why do they matter?
items.answer: memory_type classifies memories: fact (unchanging info), preference (user choices), instruction (rules to follow), relationship (people/org connections), experience (past events). This is the most powerful axis for filtering, deletion, and policy application.

items.question: How do threshold and limit work?
items.answer: limit: Number of candidate memories to retrieve (too low = miss relevant ones, too high = noisy context). threshold: 0-1 similarity cutoff (higher = stricter). Start with limit=10, threshold=0.7. If missing memories, lower threshold to 0.6 and raise limit to 20. If getting irrelevant results, raise threshold to 0.75-0.8.

items.question: Why are my search results irrelevant?
items.answer: Usually one of: 1) namespaces are mixed, 2) too many memories stored (noise), 3) threshold too low, 4) content is too abstract ('likes things' vs specific facts). Fix: separate namespaces, make content specific, raise threshold.

items.question: Search quality dropped as memories grew. What do I do?
items.answer: Add importance scoring and keep only high-importance memories. Use TTL to auto-expire old memories. Periodically merge similar memories into summaries. Separate namespaces to reduce search scope.

items.question: How should I use /api/extract?
items.answer: Recommended flow: 1) Call with auto_store=false to preview extracted memories. 2) Show results to user for confirmation. 3) Store only approved memories. 4) Once extraction quality is proven, switch to auto_store=true for automation.

items.question: What's the difference between model=haiku and model=sonnet?
items.answer: haiku: Faster, cheaper, good for most cases. sonnet: More accurate, better for important extractions (onboarding, contracts, policies). Use haiku for bulk/initial extraction, sonnet for high-stakes scenarios.

items.question: Can I modify a memory after storing it?
items.answer: Yes, but the recommended pattern is: create new memory + delete/archive old one. This approach is better for audit trails and prevents regression issues.

items.question: How do I delete memories?
items.answer: Two approaches: 1) Delete by ID (precise), 2) Delete by namespace (bulk cleanup). For enterprise/compliance, ensure 'complete deletion + audit log'. Document your deletion policy clearly.

items.question: I'm getting 429 Too Many Requests. What should I do?
items.answer: You've hit the rate limit. Solutions: 1) Implement exponential backoff (1s -> 2s -> 4s). 2) Queue requests server-side. 3) Reduce request frequency: cache duplicate queries, batch operations, lower extract frequency.

items.question: How do I reduce costs?
items.answer: Biggest cost drivers: 1) Extract frequency - reduce calls. 2) Search scope - use namespace to narrow. 3) Model choice - use haiku for routine, sonnet for important. 4) Caching - cache repeated queries. 5) Batch operations when possible.

items.question: Can I use the API key in browser (frontend)?
items.answer: Not recommended - high risk of key exposure. Call Seizn from your server (Next.js Route Handler, Cloudflare Worker, serverless function) and have the browser call your server. Never expose API keys to client-side code.

items.question: What documentation does my security/legal team need?
items.answer: They'll want: 1) Data scope (what's stored/not stored), 2) Encryption (at rest: AES-256, in transit: TLS), 3) Tenant isolation method, 4) Deletion/retention policy, 5) Audit log access, 6) Key rotation/expiration policy. Keep a Security & Governance page in your docs.

items.question: What SDKs are available?
items.answer: We provide official SDKs for TypeScript/JavaScript (npm install @seizn/sdk), Python (pip install seizn), and REST API for any language. All SDKs include TypeScript types, automatic retries, and streaming support.

items.question: What's the typical integration timeline?
items.answer: Basic integration (store/search memories) takes 1-2 hours. Adding extraction takes another 2-4 hours. Full production deployment with governance policies typically takes 1-2 weeks including testing and security review.

items.question: How do I handle multi-language content?
items.answer: Seizn's embeddings support 100+ languages. Store content in any language and search will work cross-lingually. For best results, keep a 'language' metadata field and filter by it when you want language-specific results.

items.question: Can I combine semantic search with keyword filtering?
items.answer: Yes. Use the 'filters' parameter to narrow by metadata (namespace, scope, memory_type, custom fields) before semantic ranking. This hybrid approach gives you both precision and recall.

items.question: How do I handle extraction failures?
items.answer: Check the 'status' field in the response. If extraction fails: 1) Verify input format is correct, 2) Check if content is too short (<50 chars often fails), 3) Try a different model. Set up retry logic with exponential backoff for transient errors.

items.question: Can I customize what gets extracted?
items.answer: Yes. Use the 'extraction_prompt' parameter to guide the model. You can specify: which memory types to extract, minimum importance threshold, specific fields to look for, and custom output schema.

items.question: How do I migrate from another memory/RAG system?
items.answer: Export your existing data to JSON with fields: content, metadata, created_at. Use our bulk import API (POST /api/memories/bulk) with batches of 100-500 items. Run a parallel validation query set to ensure quality matches.

items.question: What's the recommended backup strategy?
items.answer: Use the export API (GET /api/memories/export) to create periodic backups. Schedule daily exports for critical namespaces. Store exports in your own object storage (S3, GCS) with versioning enabled.

items.question: Is Seizn SOC 2 compliant?
items.answer: Yes, Seizn maintains SOC 2 Type II compliance. We also support GDPR data residency requirements with EU region deployment. Request our security questionnaire or SOC 2 report through your account dashboard.

items.question: How do I implement user data deletion for GDPR/CCPA?
items.answer: Use DELETE /api/memories with user_id filter to remove all memories for a user. Enable audit logging to prove deletion. Set retention policies to auto-delete after a period. Export user data with GET /api/memories/export?user_id=xxx for data portability requests.

componentsPage.title: UI Components
componentsPage.subtitle: Comprehensive documentation for Seizn's UI component library. Each component includes Props, usage examples, and best practices.

graphCanvas.description: Main graph canvas component using React Flow. Renders nodes and edges with interactive controls, minimap, and permission panel.

nodeRenderer.description: Custom node renderer for graph visualization. Displays node type, status, permissions with styled appearance.

edgeRenderer.description: Custom edge renderer for graph connections. Supports directed, undirected, and bidirectional edges with custom styling.

graphControls.description: Control panel for graph filtering and zoom. Filter by node type, edge type, and search.

permissionPanel.description: Side panel displaying detailed permission information for selected nodes.

explainPanel.description: Expandable panel showing full retrieval explanation including score breakdown and attributions.

scoreBreakdown.description: Visual breakdown of relevance score components (vector, keyword, rerank, recency).

attributionList.description: List of source attributions showing which documents contributed to the result.

highlightedPassage.description: Text passage with highlighted matching spans (exact match, semantic match, keyword, entity).

retOpsDashboard.description: Main dashboard for retrieval operations monitoring with auto-refresh and period selection.

metricsOverview.description: Overview cards showing key metrics: QPS, latency, cache hit rate, error rate, MRR, groundedness.

queryVolumeChart.description: Line chart showing query volume over time with search type distribution.

latencyDistribution.description: Latency percentile bar chart and trend visualization with SLO compliance indicator.

qualityTrend.description: Area chart showing search quality trends (MRR, nDCG, groundedness) with precision/recall at K.

topQueries.description: Ranked list of most frequent queries with latency, cache hit rate, and result count.

alertsPanel.description: Alert management panel with severity filtering, acknowledgment, and status tracking.

graph.title: Graph Components
graph.description: Interactive graph visualization for permission and relationship mapping

explain.title: Explain Components
explain.description: Explainability and attribution visualization for RAG results

retops.title: RetOps Components
retops.description: Retrieval operations monitoring and analytics dashboard

q4.question: When should I use scope (user/session/agent)?
q4.answer: user: Preferences that apply to a user globally. session: Goals or context valid only for this conversation. agent: Rules specific to one agent in a multi-agent system. Using scope properly reduces prompt length and improves response consistency.

q5.question: What are memory_types and why do they matter?
q5.answer: memory_type classifies memories: fact (unchanging info), preference (user choices), instruction (rules to follow), relationship (people/org connections), experience (past events). This is the most powerful axis for filtering, deletion, and policy application.

q1.question: How do threshold and limit work?
q1.answer: limit: Number of candidate memories to retrieve (too low = miss relevant ones, too high = noisy context). threshold: 0-1 similarity cutoff (higher = stricter). Start with limit=10, threshold=0.7. If missing memories, lower threshold to 0.6 and raise limit to 20. If getting irrelevant results, raise threshold to 0.75-0.8.

q2.question: Why are my search results irrelevant?
q2.answer: Usually one of: 1) namespaces are mixed, 2) too many memories stored (noise), 3) threshold too low, 4) content is too abstract ('likes things' vs specific facts). Fix: separate namespaces, make content specific, raise threshold.

q3.question: Search quality dropped as memories grew. What do I do?
q3.answer: Add importance scoring and keep only high-importance memories. Use TTL to auto-expire old memories. Periodically merge similar memories into summaries. Separate namespaces to reduce search scope.

q1.question: How should I use /api/extract?
q1.answer: Recommended flow: 1) Call with auto_store=false to preview extracted memories. 2) Show results to user for confirmation. 3) Store only approved memories. 4) Once extraction quality is proven, switch to auto_store=true for automation.

q2.question: What's the difference between model=haiku and model=sonnet?
q2.answer: haiku: Faster, cheaper, good for most cases. sonnet: More accurate, better for important extractions (onboarding, contracts, policies). Use haiku for bulk/initial extraction, sonnet for high-stakes scenarios.

q1.question: Can I modify a memory after storing it?
q1.answer: Yes, but the recommended pattern is: create new memory + delete/archive old one. This approach is better for audit trails and prevents regression issues.

q2.question: How do I delete memories?
q2.answer: Two approaches: 1) Delete by ID (precise), 2) Delete by namespace (bulk cleanup). For enterprise/compliance, ensure 'complete deletion + audit log'. Document your deletion policy clearly.

q3.question: I'm getting 429 Too Many Requests. What should I do?
q3.answer: You've hit the rate limit. Solutions: 1) Implement exponential backoff (1s → 2s → 4s). 2) Queue requests server-side. 3) Reduce request frequency: cache duplicate queries, batch operations, lower extract frequency.

q4.question: How do I reduce costs?
q4.answer: Biggest cost drivers: 1) Extract frequency - reduce calls. 2) Search scope - use namespace to narrow. 3) Model choice - use haiku for routine, sonnet for important. 4) Caching - cache repeated queries. 5) Batch operations when possible.

q1.question: Can I use the API key in browser (frontend)?
q1.answer: Not recommended - high risk of key exposure. Call Seizn from your server (Next.js Route Handler, Cloudflare Worker, serverless function) and have the browser call your server. Never expose API keys to client-side code.

q2.question: What documentation does my security/legal team need?
q2.answer: They'll want: 1) Data scope (what's stored/not stored), 2) Encryption (at rest: AES-256, in transit: TLS), 3) Tenant isolation method, 4) Deletion/retention policy, 5) Audit log access, 6) Key rotation/expiration policy. Keep a Security & Governance page in your docs.
